title: The Bride! is a bold, bloody, feminist Frankenstein freakout
description: Maggie Gyllenhaal and Jessie Buckley mean to make you uncomfortable in their confrontational monster movie The Bride!
hook: Gyllenhaal and Buckley's feminist Frankenstein remake confronts creation, abandonment, and female monstrosity with unflinching provocation.

title: A Novel That’s Part Manifesto, Part Unauthorized Fictional Memoir
description:
Night Night Fawn is without a doubt the Marxist, trans, comedic dystopia we need in 2026. Initially conceived as nonfiction, Jordy Rosenberg’s second novel subverts form to become an inherently transgressive, unauthorized, fictional “memoir” that reads as hysterical manifesto.  Barbara Rosenberg, a character modeled loosely on Rosenberg’s own mother, is a terminally ill Jewish “yenta.” High on […]

The post
hook: Jordy Rosenberg's transgressive novel blurs manifesto and memoir—a Marxist, trans, comedic dystopia disguised as unauthorized fiction.

title: Mundane, magic, maybe both — a new book explores 'The Writer's Room'
description: Why are we captivated by the spaces where authors write? Katie da Cunha Lewin set out to explore "The Hidden Worlds That Shape the Books We Love."
hook: Katie da Cunha Lewin explores the hidden spaces where authors write and how physical environments shape the books we love.

title: Claire Lynch wins Nero Gold prize for debut about 1980s homophobia
description:
The £30,000 award went to novel A Family Matter, about a lesbian affair and a custody battle

A debut novel exploring the long-term effects of prejudice and secrecy on a lesbian couple in the 1980s has won the Nero Gold prize.

Claire Lynch was presented with the £30,000 award for her book A Family Matter at a ceremony in London on Wednesday evening.

Continue reading...
hook: Claire Lynch's debut traces a lesbian affair and custody battle through 1980s homophobia, examining how prejudice warps intimacy across decades.

title: Dreamwalking and the Book of Eywa
description: In James Cameron’s dreamworld of stereoscopic sight, he’s always centered the act of seeing. Just watch the opening and closing shots; first, as the camera plunges through a magical rainforest wreathed in fog, a spectacle that mind-melds the film’s point of view with our own. And last, in a close-up of newly reopened eyes, breaking the fourth wall as they gaze right at us. It’s the visual equivalent of “I see you,” the Na’vi phrase of deep, spiritual bonding.
hook: How James Cameron uses spectacle and seeing itself as meaning—a deep visual analysis of Avatar's dreamworld and cinematic philosophy.
